title: Adaptive Multi-model Fusion Learning for Sparse-Reward Reinforcement Learning

reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This paper proposes an intrinsic reward formulation to address the challenge of sparse reward in reinforcement learning. The key idea is to learn multiple models. The prediction error of each model is used as a component of the intrinsic reward. These components are fused using an alpha-mean function. The parameter alpha can be tuned automatically throughout the training process using meta-gradient methods. The method is evaluated on 6 OpenAI continuous control benchmarks (with delayed reward), and demonstrates better performance than several state-of-the-art prior works on intrinsic reward.

Overall, the paper is tackling an important challenge of reinforcement learning. Thus its potential impact is large. The paper is also well written and the results are promising. My main concern is that one important baseline is missing: [Pathak 2019]: "Self-supervised exploration via disagreement." This prior work is very relevant. It also used multiple models and the implementation was so much simpler than the proposed method in this paper. In addition, this paper also claims to be a generalization of [Pathak 2019]. Thus, adding [Pathak 2019] into the baselines for comparisons seems necessary.

I am also curious why not use MSE to measure the model-prediction error? The KL Divergence used in this paper introduces a lot of complexities, both in derivation and implementation (e.g. eq. 9, 10, 11). If MSE is not compatible with the proposed method, more discussions in the paper is welcome. Otherwise, adding an ablation to compare MSE with KLD would help, especially if KLD yields better results than MSE. This would justify the extra complexity introduced by using KLD.

The need of Condition 2 in Section 3.3 is not sufficiently motivated. How does this condition affect derivation, implementation and final results? It would be great to have a few sentences to clarity this.

In summary, this is a good paper. However, missing an important baseline [Pathak 2019] seems a significant oversight. I hope that this baseline will be added in the future version of this paper.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
Review:

This manuscript introduces a novel intrinsic reward mechanism to mitigate the sparse-reward problem in reinforcement learning. The approach involves training an ensemble of predictive models and using each model’s prediction error as a distinct intrinsic reward signal. These error signals are then combined via an α-mean aggregation, where the parameter α is adaptively adjusted during training through meta-gradient optimization. The authors validate their technique on six OpenAI continuous-control tasks with delayed rewards, showing improvements over several leading intrinsic-reward methods.

Strengths:
- Addresses the critical issue of exploration under sparse rewards, which has significant implications for RL research.
- Presents a clear and coherent write-up, with empirical results that appear robust and encouraging.
- The meta-gradient tuning of α is an elegant solution that automates a key hyperparameter.

Major Concern:
- An essential baseline is missing: Pathak et al. (2019), “Self-supervised exploration via disagreement.” That work also leverages multiple models—albeit with a much simpler implementation—and the current paper positions itself as a generalization of it. Including Pathak (2019) in the experimental comparison is crucial to substantiate the claimed advance.

Additional Suggestions:
1. Choice of Error Metric: The paper employs KL divergence to quantify model-prediction error, which introduces analytic and implementation overhead (e.g., Eqs. 9–11). If mean-squared error (MSE) is incompatible with the proposed framework, the manuscript should discuss this explicitly. Otherwise, a brief ablation contrasting MSE and KL-divergence-based errors would strengthen the justification for the extra complexity.
2. Condition 2 in Section 3.3: The rationale for imposing this condition is not sufficiently explained. A few sentences detailing its impact on the theoretical derivation, practical implementation, and empirical outcomes would improve clarity.

Conclusion:
This is a solid contribution with promising results and high potential impact. However, omitting Pathak et al. (2019) from the baselines is a notable oversight. I strongly encourage the authors to include that comparison in a revised version.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept

reviews.confidence: 3: The reviewer is fairly confident that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
In this paper, the authors explore a model based intrinsic reward generation mechanism, in environment settings where the reward assignment is sparse. The authors used an ensemble of models,  and computed the alpha-mean value of their KL divergences with respect to the "true transitions". The alpha-mean serves as the intrinsic reward, with the parameter alpha being co-optimized during the training.  The authors demonstrated that their proposed approach yields top performance in six augmented MuJuCo continuous control tasks. 


Pros:

(1) A clearly written paper and easy to read and understand. 

(2) The approach demonstrated consistent top performance in all benchmarks.  

Cons:

(1) Although the author provides a few ablation studies. I propose to add at least one more study that compares the effectiveness of their method under different "sparsity" settings. In all experiments, the authors used a fixed interval of 40, at which extrinsic rewards are computed. I am interested to see if their conclusion can still hold when this interval is set at, for example, 10, 20, 60, etc.

(2) The alpha-mean method is one type of scale invariant transformation. I wonder if the authors have studied other transformation functions, or even scale invariant kernel functions?

(3) There are other sparse-reward tasks such as pushing/sliding/pick-and-place, which are under a different category than MuJuCo continuous control tasks. I am interested in seeing if the proposed approach can still perform in these settings. This will also help make the author's statements more convincing. 

Conclusion:
Please address my concerns raised in the "cons" section.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review:
This manuscript investigates a model-based approach for generating intrinsic rewards in environments with sparse external feedback. The authors deploy an ensemble of predictive models and calculate the α-mean of their KL divergences relative to the true transition dynamics. This α-mean value, with α learned alongside the policy, serves as the intrinsic reward signal. Empirical results on six augmented MuJoCo continuous-control benchmarks indicate that this method consistently achieves leading performance.

Strengths:

1. The paper is well structured and clearly communicated, making it straightforward to follow the methodology and experiments.
2. Across all reported benchmarks, the proposed scheme secures top-tier results, demonstrating its effectiveness.

Weaknesses:

1. While the authors include several ablation analyses, they only evaluate a fixed extrinsic-reward sparsity interval of 40 steps. It would strengthen the study to see how performance varies when this interval is altered—for instance, to 10, 20, or 60 steps—to verify the method’s robustness under different sparsity conditions.
2. The α-mean aggregation is one particular scale-invariant transformation. It would be valuable to know whether alternative transformations or scale-invariant kernel functions were considered or could yield similar or improved outcomes.
3. The evaluation is limited to MuJoCo continuous-control tasks. Assessing the approach on other sparse-reward challenges—such as pushing, sliding, or pick-and-place tasks—would broaden its applicability and reinforce the authors’ claims.

Conclusion:
I recommend acceptance, provided the authors address the concerns outlined above, particularly by expanding their ablations on sparsity intervals, exploring other transformation functions, and testing on additional sparse-reward domains.

reviews.confidence: 3: The reviewer is fairly confident that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
In this paper, the authors present a generalization of the model-prediction-error-based intrinsic reward method by fusing predictions from multiple models.
The authors considered the sparse reward scenario in reinforcement learning.

In related work, the authors mentioned that previous works on image spaces are not directly related to theirs. However, I did not understand what are the limitations or the caveats of the proposed method that leads to this conclusion.

Also in related work, I did not understand the purpose of detailing not related work in Sec. 2.2., I believe that the authors could use this space to discuss, for instance,
applications of fusion methods in reinforcement learning scenarios, such as: “Data fusion using Bayesian theory and reinforcement learning method”.
Another option, even better, is discussing approaches proposed in the line of investigation using ensembles
(e.g. “Model Ensemble-Based Intrinsic Reward for Sparse Reward Reinforcement Learning”).

I would like to understand the computational costs involved in using such a fusion approach., both in terms of individual methods and alpha optimization.

In Fig. 2, it is hard to conclude that the gain over Module baseline is significant only considering the figure. I don’t know if the authors are considering some
statistical test when they mention significance, if this is the case, they should properly present the test and premisses.

In Sec.4.2.4, it is hard to see how the performance improves as K increases. First, only four values for K are a limitation to conclude this. 
Additionally, for instance, when considering the Walker2d dataset, the performance for K=2 is better than K=3.

References should point to the published work rather than the arxiv entries, when the former is available (e..g. Exploration by random network distillation, ICLR’19).

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review:

This manuscript extends model-prediction-error–based intrinsic rewards by integrating forecasts from multiple predictive models, focusing on reinforcement learning tasks with sparse external feedback. While the overall idea is promising, several points need clarification or improvement:

1. Related Work – Image-Space Methods  
   The authors assert that prior approaches operating in image spaces are not directly applicable to their method, but they do not explain what specific shortcomings or assumptions of those image-based techniques justify this claim. A brief discussion of the limitations or caveats of the proposed fusion strategy—especially in contrast to image-space predictions—would help readers understand why existing work cannot be applied.

2. Related Work – Section 2.2  
   Section 2.2 lists work deemed “not related,” yet its space could be better used to survey fusion techniques within reinforcement learning. For example, the authors might describe “Data fusion using Bayesian theory in reinforcement learning” or methods in the ensemble-based intrinsic reward literature (e.g., “Model Ensemble-Based Intrinsic Reward for Sparse Reward Reinforcement Learning”). This comparison would position the paper more clearly within current research trends.

3. Computational Cost Analysis  
   It is unclear what the runtime and resource overheads are for combining multiple models in the proposed manner. The manuscript should report the computational cost of each base predictor as well as the overhead introduced by tuning or optimizing the fusion weight α.

4. Statistical Significance in Figure 2  
   Figure 2 is used to claim that the fusion approach significantly outperforms the single-model baseline, but the plot alone does not convince. If the authors have performed formal significance testing, they must specify the test used, its assumptions, and the resulting p-values or confidence intervals.

5. Behavior as K Increases (Section 4.2.4)  
   The text suggests that performance improves with larger ensemble size K, yet only four K values are evaluated, limiting generality. Moreover, in the Walker2d experiment, K=2 yields better results than K=3, contradicting the claimed monotonic trend. A broader range of K or a discussion of these anomalies is needed.

6. Reference Formatting  
   When a paper has been published in a conference or journal, please cite that version rather than the arXiv preprint. For instance, the Exploration by Random Network Distillation work should reference ICLR 2019 rather than its arXiv entry.

Overall, the paper introduces an interesting extension of prediction-error intrinsic rewards, but it requires deeper contextualization in related work, clearer analysis of computational and statistical aspects, and more thorough experimentation to support its claims.

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold

reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
Summary
The papers looks at the problem of using intrinsic rewards to help agent explore in sparse reward settings. The paper proposes combining multiple intrinsic rewards and proposes a meta-gradient based method to learning the fusion of these intrinsic rewards.


Strengths
1. Learning in a sparse rewards setting is a challenging and relevant problem to the community.
2. The paper is well written 
3. The idea of learning to use multiple intrinsic rewards and using different combinations of them at different times seems to be helpful in the experiments

Weaknesses/Comments/Questions
1. My main concern is that the contribution of the paper might not be sufficient. The main contribution claimed by the paper to me seems like the idea of combining different intrinsic rewards and also learning the hyper-parameter that controls the fusion. In practice different intrinsic rewards are indeed used together and varied at different times. Also learning RL related hyperparameters using meta-gradient (Meta-gradient RL) is not new.
2. I don't think Zheng et al is really meant for sparse reward settings. So not sure if that's the right SOTA baseline to compare with. Zheng et al is not expected to learn intrinsic rewards that guide initial exploration as it needs extrinsic feedback to learn these intrinsic rewards, else they are just random.
3. It would be good if the paper evaluates on some domains that are naturally sparse reward setting instead of converting a dense reward setting to a sparse reward setting by accumulating and delaying rewards.
4. If the main contribution of the paper is on how to fuse, then simple methods of fusing like learning a lenear combination or just a single layer neural network over the different model prediction errors to output the intrinsic reward to use all seem reasonable. Comparison with those would be good.
5. The conditions that lead to the particular choice of fusion should be verified using ablation study to see if they indeed lead to the performance boost expected or if the performance comes from something else.
6. Clarification: Is it that all the experiments only fuse between two intrinsic rewards (prediction errors)? If the paper is about the importance of combining different prediction errors, it would be nice to have more than 2 and also some interestingly different model predictions and also analysis and comparison on what happens if we just use them separately. 

Questions to authors.
1. Please respond to the comments above. Thanks.

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review:

Summary  
This paper tackles exploration under sparse reward conditions by leveraging intrinsic motivation signals. Specifically, the authors propose a method that blends multiple intrinsic reward functions and uses meta-gradient techniques to learn how to weight these signals over time.

Strengths  
1. Exploration in sparse-reward environments is a significant challenge and of high interest to the community.  
2. The manuscript is clearly written and easy to follow.  
3. Empirically, the adaptive combination of intrinsic rewards appears beneficial, as shown in the experiments.

Weaknesses / Comments / Questions  
1. The novelty of the contribution is somewhat limited. Combining various intrinsic rewards and tuning their fusion parameter via meta-gradients seems incremental, since practitioners already mix intrinsic bonuses in practice, and applying meta-gradient methods to tune RL hyperparameters is established.  
2. Zheng et al. is not designed for sparse-reward tasks. It relies on extrinsic signals to shape its learned intrinsic rewards and thus may be an inappropriate state-of-the-art comparison for initial exploration without external feedback.  
3. The evaluation domains are constructed by sparsifying originally dense-reward tasks. It would strengthen the paper to test on environments that are inherently sparse in their natural formulation.  
4. If the key claim is about the fusion mechanism, then benchmarking against simpler fusion schemes—such as a learned linear weighting or a one-layer neural aggregator of prediction errors—would be informative.  
5. An ablation study is needed to confirm that the specific fusion approach drives the performance gains, rather than other factors.  
6. It is unclear whether experiments only mix two intrinsic error signals. For a paper emphasizing the value of combining multiple prediction errors, it would be valuable to include more than two sources, explore diverse model predictions, and analyze outcomes when using each source independently.

Questions for the authors  
Please address the points above and clarify the experimental setup regarding the number and types of intrinsic rewards used. Thank you.

title: A Unified Framework for Convolution-based Graph Neural Networks

reviews.confidence: 3: The reviewer is fairly confident that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
The paper introduces a unified framework for graph convolutional networks by interpreting filters as regularizers in the graph Fourier domain.
In particular, this framework allows to establish the relationships between standard, attention-based and topology-based GNNs.
Furthermore, the authors propose a regularization technique based upon the proposed framework which tackles the oversmoothing problem of GNNs, which achieves clear benefits on standard (small) benchmark datasets.

The paper is mostly well-written, although though to understand on first read.
I especially liked that it tries to establish a systematic view of different GNN models and their relations, which is a welcome work in the field of graph representation learning (especially with the sheer amount of GNN models available in literature).
In my opinion, the proposed framework has the potential to improve our understanding of GCNs and inspire better models in return.

On the other hand, it is not exactly clear to me how the proposed regularization technique differs from PairNorm (which is build upon similar insights by preventing node embeddings from becoming too similar). I would very much welcome a discussion between key differences and similarities between the two approaches. Furthermore, the authors should consider comparing the proposed regularization technique against related approaches, e.g., PairNorm and DropEdge.
Overall, the empirical evaluation feels a bit shallow by only evaluating on small benchmark datasets, but might be sufficient for a work that has mostly theoretical contributions.

Minor comments:

* It is not exactly clear to me how ECC can be viewed as an attention-based GNN since this operator learns a weight matrix conditioned on the edge features (instead of performing weighted normalization). Does this operator really fit into the proposed unified framework?

* A GCN baseline is missing on the PPI dataset.

============== Post Rebuttal Comments =================

I would like to thank the authors for their insightful rebuttal and clarifications. Sadly, I cannot find the newly added section regarding the non-linearity analysis in the revised manuscript and therefore cannot judge the findings of the authors.

Hence, my rating will stay the same.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
Review:

This manuscript presents a unified perspective on graph convolutional networks by casting convolutional filters as regularizers in the graph Fourier domain. Within this framework, the authors clarify how conventional GCNs, attention-driven GNNs, and topology-focused models are interrelated. Building on these insights, they introduce a regularization scheme to alleviate the oversmoothing phenomenon in deep GNNs, demonstrating noticeable performance gains on common small-scale benchmark datasets.

Strengths  
1. The paper is generally well composed, albeit somewhat dense on an initial reading.  
2. I appreciate the authors’ effort to organize the diverse landscape of GNN variants under one coherent theoretical umbrella—a valuable contribution given the proliferation of models in graph representation learning.  
3. The framework has real promise to deepen our theoretical grasp of GCN behaviors and to spark the design of more effective graph-based learning architectures.

Weaknesses and Suggestions  
1. The manuscript does not clearly delineate how the proposed regularizer differs from existing methods such as PairNorm, which similarly aims to prevent node embeddings from converging to overly similar representations. I recommend the authors include a focused discussion contrasting the mechanics and theoretical underpinnings of their approach versus PairNorm.  
2. In addition to PairNorm, it would strengthen the experimental section to benchmark against other recent oversmoothing mitigations, for example DropEdge.  
3. The empirical evaluation is limited to small, standard benchmarks. While this may be sufficient for a primarily theoretical contribution, expanding experiments or discussing scalability to larger or more varied datasets would enhance confidence in the method’s generality.

Minor Comments  
- It is not immediately obvious how the ECC operator conforms to the proposed attention-based category, since ECC generates weight matrices conditioned on edge features rather than performing a weighted normalization akin to typical attention mechanisms. Clarification on this point would be helpful.  
- On the PPI dataset, a vanilla GCN baseline is omitted—this comparison should be added.

Post-Rebuttal Remarks  
I appreciate the authors’ responses and clarifications. However, I was unable to locate the newly mentioned section on non-linearity analysis in the revised submission, and so cannot assess those findings. Consequently, my recommendation remains unchanged.

Decision: 7: Good paper, accept
reviews.original_text:
Review:
The paper introduces a unified framework for graph convolutional networks by interpreting filters as regularizers in the graph Fourier domain.
In particular, this framework allows to establish the relationships between standard, attention-based and topology-based GNNs.
Furthermore, the authors propose a regularization technique based upon the proposed framework which tackles the oversmoothing problem of GNNs, which achieves clear benefits on standard (small) benchmark datasets.

The paper is mostly well-written, although though to understand on first read.
I especially liked that it tries to establish a systematic view of different GNN models and their relations, which is a welcome work in the field of graph representation learning (especially with the sheer amount of GNN models available in literature).
In my opinion, the proposed framework has the potential to improve our understanding of GCNs and inspire better models in return.

On the other hand, it is not exactly clear to me how the proposed regularization technique differs from PairNorm (which is build upon similar insights by preventing node embeddings from becoming too similar). I would very much welcome a discussion between key differences and similarities between the two approaches. Furthermore, the authors should consider comparing the proposed regularization technique against related approaches, e.g., PairNorm and DropEdge.
Overall, the empirical evaluation feels a bit shallow by only evaluating on small benchmark datasets, but might be sufficient for a work that has mostly theoretical contributions.

Minor comments:

* It is not exactly clear to me how ECC can be viewed as an attention-based GNN since this operator learns a weight matrix conditioned on the edge features (instead of performing weighted normalization). Does this operator really fit into the proposed unified framework?

* A GCN baseline is missing on the PPI dataset.

============== Post Rebuttal Comments =================

I would like to thank the authors for their insightful rebuttal and clarifications. Sadly, I cannot find the newly added section regarding the non-linearity analysis in the revised manuscript and therefore cannot judge the findings of the authors.

Hence, my rating will stay the same.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept

reviews.confidence: 3: The reviewer is fairly confident that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This paper unifies several variants of the graph convolutional networks (GCNs) into a regularized quadratic optimization framework. Basically, the function to be optimized considers both to preserve node information and to perform graph Laplacian regularization, whose optimal solution gives a convolutional layer.

The unification is given by equations (3) and (18) and elaborated in section 3, which includes several methods including GCN, graph attentions, residual connections, concatenation, etc. This is not surprising: as a GCN layer (without activation) is a linear transformation, surely it is the optimum of a quadratic function. Broadly, any linear layer can be trivially formulated as a quadratic optimization problem. Still, I appreciate the authors' delicate work on unifying these diverse methods from an optimization perspective, which is useful and could lead to new methods.

From a technical perspective, the main novelty is that the authors further extend this framework by adding another feature variance term, so that the learned features have a certain variance. This is similar to the idea of batch normalization. This is reasonable because GCN tends to correlate the learned features with the graph Laplacian embedding (the optimal solution of the 2nd term in the authors' framework).

This is interesting but empirical. I would like to see how this additional regularization can be equivalent to transforming the original graph with some formal arguments. Unfortunately, this technic is mainly introduced as a heuristic and more detailed analysis is missing.

As in any regularization framework, there is an additional parameter involved that is the regularization strength (\alpha_3 in 21). Therefore the performance improvement is not surprising as the model is "enlarged". In the experiments or supplementary material, there should be a sensitivity study of this parameter.

On three citation graphs (that are commonly used to evaluate graph neural networks) and semi-supervised node classification tasks, the authors showed that the regularizer can bring marginal performance improvement.

Regarding Clarity, there are some typos in several places and rarely used phrases.

Overall, I don't feel excited after reading the article (although the contents are useful), as a large part of this work is on summarizing existing literature.  The "new bit" is mainly on the additional regularization term that is introduced as a heuristic.

Based on the novelty, a more proper venue for publishing this work could be relevant journals. Overall this submission presents a borderline case and I recommend weak acceptance.

As a minor comment: Equation (21) why not set \alpha_1=1?

----
After rebuttal:

Novelty: my assessment remains the same. It is not non-trivial enough to combine several linear operators into a unified optimization framework. Although the unification is useful, it is not a major novelty.

Thank you for the additional experiments on testing the hyper-parameter. As you mentioned instability, it is worth to have some toy example to demonstrate the instability and study the cause of such instability and show how to avoid such instability using the proposed regularizer. Clearly (19) is bounded. When \alpha_3 is large enough, the solution will be trivial.

Regarding non-linearity: the authors' framework is for unifying a graph convolution operator (that is one layer in a graph neural network). Nonlinear activation is another operator. This is not a major problem from my perspective.

Overall, I think this work has some value (although the novelty is not strong) and still recommend weak acceptance.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review:

This manuscript presents a framework that casts various graph convolutional network (GCN) formulations into a single regularized quadratic optimization problem. In essence, the objective balances fidelity to the original node features with a Laplacian-smoothing penalty, and its closed-form minimizer yields a convolutional layer.

The core unification—spelled out in Equations (3) and (18) and discussed in Section 3—encompasses multiple existing approaches, from vanilla GCNs to graph attention, residual links, and feature concatenation. While it is insightful to see these linear layers interpreted as the optimum of a quadratic criterion, this result is unsurprising: any linear transformation (absent a nonlinearity) can be recast as solving a quadratic problem. Nonetheless, I appreciate the systematic consolidation of these methods under an optimization lens, which may inspire future developments.

The paper’s main technical contribution is the introduction of a third term enforcing a target variance on the learned features—an idea reminiscent of batch normalization. This addition is motivated by the tendency of GCNs to overly align learned representations with the graph Laplacian’s eigenstructure. Although plausible, this new regularizer is currently justified only heuristically; a more formal treatment showing its equivalence to some graph transformation would strengthen the argument.

As with any additional regularization, a new hyperparameter (\alpha₃ in Equation 21) is introduced. Given that enlarging the model capacity naturally can boost performance, I would expect a sensitivity analysis for this parameter in either the main text or the supplement to assess robustness.

Empirically, experiments on three standard citation networks for semi-supervised node classification reveal only modest gains from the proposed variance term. The improvements, while consistent, are marginal.

Regarding presentation, there are a few typographical errors and instances of awkward phrasing that warrant cleanup.

Overall, the paper offers a useful summary of existing GCN variants and supplements it with a variance-based regularizer. However, a substantial portion of the work is a rephrasing of known methods, and the novel component remains heuristic without deeper theory. Thus, while the contribution has value, it stops short of being a major advance. A journal venue might be more fitting, but as is, this submission hovers just above the bar. I recommend a weak acceptance (Rating: 6).

Minor point: In Equation (21), why not fix \alpha₁ = 1?

—After Rebuttal—

My view on novelty remains unchanged: unifying linear graph operators via quadratic programming, though tidy, does not constitute a strong theoretical breakthrough. The added experiments on hyperparameter sensitivity are appreciated, though it would be even more convincing to include a simple illustrative example demonstrating the instability you noted, analyze its source, and show how the new regularizer mitigates it. Indeed, when \alpha₃ grows large, the solution trivially collapses, so a toy case could illuminate this behavior.

On the matter of nonlinear activations: since the framework is specifically unifying the convolutional (linear) step, treating activations separately is acceptable and not a critical flaw.

In sum, this work contributes a coherent unification and a heuristic variance penalty that yield small empirical benefits. Its novelty is modest, but it does provide useful insight, so I maintain my recommendation of weak acceptance.

reviews.confidence: 5: The reviewer is absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct and very familiar with the relevant literature
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This paper presents a unified framework for graph convolutional neural networks based on regularized optimization, connecting different  variants of graph neural networks including vanilla, attention-based, and topology-based approaches. The authors also propose  a novel regularization technique to approach the oversmoothing problem in graph convolution. Experiments on the standard settings of node classification on Citeseer, Cora, and Pubmed prove the effectiveness of the proposed regularization techniques. 

Overall, this is a very interesting paper, proposing a unified framework for different variants of convolution-based graph neural networks. However, I also have a few concerns:

(1) The proposed framework is mainly designed for GNNs without considering the nonlinear transformation matrix. What if we have to consider the nonlinear transformation? Is the whole framework able to unify different GNNs?

(2) In the case of linear GNNs (without nonlinear transformation matrix), it is actually not surprising formulating GNNs as a regularized optimization problem. Such a regularization framework has already been discussed in the original GCN paper (Kipf et al. 2016). 

(3) In the case of linear GNNs, the overall framework is also very similar to the traditional label propagation framework (Zhou et al. Learning with Local and Global Consistency). Could you explain the difference?

(4) The new novel regularization technique seems to be similar to the one proposed in PairNorm (Zhao et al. 2020). Could you also explain the difference?

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
This manuscript introduces a unified perspective on graph convolutional neural networks by framing them as regularized optimization problems. In doing so, it draws connections among several GNN variants—standard convolutional, attention-driven, and topology-oriented methods—and further proposes a new regularization strategy aimed at mitigating the well-known oversmoothing issue in graph convolutions. The authors validate their approach through node classification experiments on the Citeseer, Cora, and Pubmed benchmarks, demonstrating that their regularizer yields performance improvements over baseline models.

While the paper tackles an important topic and offers an appealing high-level framework, I have a number of reservations:

1. Scope limited to linear GNNs.  
   The framework is developed under the assumption that the GNN layers lack any nonlinear transformation matrices. In practice, however, most successful architectures include nonlinearities and learnable transformations. How would the proposed unification extend when such nonlinear projection steps are introduced? Can the authors show that their framework still captures the behavior of these more general models?

2. Novelty of the regularized formulation.  
   Casting a linear GNN as a regularized optimization problem is not entirely unexpected—in fact, the original GCN formulation by Kipf et al. (2016) already discusses a smoothing-based regularization viewpoint. It would help to clarify precisely what’s new here beyond restating that a GNN objective can be seen through a regularizer.

3. Similarity to label propagation.  
   The overall structure for the linear case bears a strong resemblance to classical label propagation methods (e.g., Zhou et al.’s Learning with Local and Global Consistency). The authors should explicitly compare and contrast their unified framework with that canonical approach to highlight any substantive distinctions.

4. Relationship to PairNorm.  
   The newly proposed regularization term to address oversmoothing seems closely related to the normalization strategy in PairNorm (Zhao et al., 2020). Please elaborate on how your technique differs from or improves upon that prior work.

Recommendation: 5: Marginally below acceptance threshold.

reviews.confidence: 3: The reviewer is fairly confident that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
Summary: The paper shows that several graph networks (GCN, attention GCN, PPNP, residual) can be unified under a common framework of Laplacian-regularised optimisation. Subsequently, different types of regularisation are combined to propose a new method for graph transduction, which is then empirically evaluated.

Significance: Laplacian regularisation is a classical approach for formulating/justifying graph transduction algorithms (multiple papers by Mikhail Belkin and Xiaojin Zhu around 2004-06). It is interesting to see that so many graph networks can also be unified in the same framework. A unified framework does aid in both theoretical analysis and implementation of GCNs.
However, the claims and derivation do not seem to account for the non-linear activation in the networks, and hence, significance of the work seems limited.

Quality: As noted above, non-linearity is not considered which makes the derivation significantly simpler. Moreover, the first-order approximation is quite misleading since even the proof do not seem to consider non-linear activation. 
Since the proposed method combines multiple types of regularisation, it is expected to perform better than other networks. However, it is not clear if the training time increases due to the complex regularisation.

Clarity and orginality: The paper is otherwise well written / organised, and the theoretical contributions (although technically straightforward) seem original and somewhat interesting.

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review:

Summary  
This manuscript demonstrates that a variety of graph-based networks—including standard GCNs, attention-augmented GCNs, PPNP, and residual GCNs—can be derived from a unified perspective of Laplacian-regularized optimization. Building on this unification, the authors introduce a novel graph transduction algorithm by integrating several regularization terms, and they provide empirical results to validate its performance.

Significance  
Laplacian regularization has long been a foundational tool for justifying graph transduction techniques (notably in the work of Belkin and Zhu, mid-2000s). It is therefore valuable to see that many modern graph neural architectures can be interpreted within the same framework, which may facilitate both their theoretical understanding and implementation. However, the paper’s derivations appear to omit the effect of non-linear activation functions in these networks. This omission limits the broader impact and generality of the theoretical unification.

Quality  
The exclusion of non-linearities makes the mathematical derivations considerably simpler than what would be needed for real-world GCNs. Furthermore, the reliance on a first-order approximation is somewhat misleading, since the proofs do not actually incorporate the activations. While the proposed approach—by virtue of combining multiple regularizers—naturally achieves strong empirical performance, the authors do not discuss whether this added regularization leads to increased training time or computational overhead.

Clarity and Originality  
Aside from the issues above, the paper is clearly written and well organized. The theoretical perspective, though technically straightforward, is an original contribution and holds some interest for researchers studying the foundations of graph neural networks.

Rating  
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold

title: Practical Marginalized Importance Sampling with the Successor Representation

reviews.confidence: 3: The reviewer is fairly confident that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
***Summary***
The paper proposes an approach to employ successor representation combined with marginalized importance sampling. The basic idea exploited in the paper consists of expressing the occupancies in terms of the successor representation and to model it via a linear combination of some features. This allows handling, although approximately, continuous state-action spaces. After having derived the objective function, an experimental evaluation on both Mujoco and Atari domains is presented, including an ablation study.

***Major***
- (About the linearity of the weight) Linear representations expressed in terms of a feature function are common in RL as the reward function can be often seen as a trade-off of different objectives encoded in the features. However, the choice of the linear representation in Equation (7) is based on the assumption that the marginalized weight is linear in the feature function. This assumption seems to me less justified compared to the one for the reward function. Clearly, a suitable feature design could overcome this limitation. Can the authors explain how the features \phi are selected or learned? 
- (Experimental evaluation) The results presented in the experimental evaluation are partially unsatisfactory, as also the authors acknowledge. It seems that there is no clear benefit in employing the marginalized importances sampling (both the baselines and the proposed approach) compared to standard deep temporal difference approaches. The authors suggest that this phenomenon can be ascribed to the fact that the quality of the marginalized weights is affected by the successor representation learned. I don't think this is the main weakness of the paper, but a reflection of the usefulness of the method in complex scenarios is necessary. Alternatively, it would be interesting to compare the proposed approach with DualDICE and GradientDICE on simpler tasks (maybe toy ones) in which DualDICE and GradientDICE work well.


***Minor***
- The related work section should be moved later in the paper, maybe after Section 4
- Pag 2, two lines above Equation (2): the transition model is here employed as a distribution over the next state s' and the reward r, but the reward function is considered separately in the definition of MDP presented before
- Figures 2, 3, and 4: the plots are not readable when printing the paper in grayscale. I suggest using different linestyles and/or markers

***Typos***
- Pag 2: isn't -> is not
- Pag 2: doesn't -> does not
- Pag 8: the the -> the

***Overall***
The paper can be considered incremental compared to DualDICE. I did not find any fault, but I feel that the significance of contribution is currently insufficient for publication at ICLR. In particular, for a paper that proposes a practical variation of a theoretically sound algorithm, the experimental evaluation is essential. I think that the results are currently unable to clearly show the advantages of the proposed method.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review:

Summary  
This work introduces a method that merges successor representations with marginalized importance sampling. The authors reformulate state-action occupancies through the successor representation and approximate it using a linear combination of feature vectors. This strategy extends to continuous state-action spaces. After deriving the learning objective, the paper reports empirical results on both MuJoCo and Atari benchmarks, including an ablation analysis.

Major Concerns  
1. Linearity Assumption in Equation (7)  
   • While linear feature-based models are widespread in RL—often justified by decomposing rewards into interpretable components—the paper’s assumption that the marginalized importance weight is itself a linear function of φ seems less supported.  
   • A richer discussion is needed on how the feature mapping φ is chosen or optimized. Could the authors clarify the process for selecting or learning these features to ensure that the linear approximation is reasonable?  

2. Experimental Validation  
   • As the authors note, the empirical gains from marginalized importance sampling (both in the baseline variants and the proposed algorithm) over standard deep TD methods are not evident.  
   • The manuscript attributes this lack of improvement to errors in the learned successor representation affecting weight quality. While plausible, I believe a deeper examination of the method’s practical utility in challenging domains is warranted.  
   • As an alternative—or in addition—comparing performance against DualDICE and GradientDICE on simpler tasks (where those approaches perform well) could offer clearer insights into when and why the proposed approach succeeds or fails.  

Minor Issues  
• I suggest moving the Related Work section to a later position in the paper (e.g., after Section 4) for better narrative flow.  
• On page 2, two lines above Equation (2), the transition kernel is treated as a joint distribution over next state s′ and reward r, yet earlier the reward was defined separately in the MDP formulation; please reconcile these descriptions.  
• In Figures 2–4, the curves become indistinguishable when printed in grayscale. Consider using varied line styles or markers to improve legibility.  

Typographical Corrections  
• Page 2: “isn’t” → “is not”  
• Page 2: “doesn’t” → “does not”  
• Page 8: remove the duplicate “the” (“the the” → “the”)  

Overall Assessment  
This submission represents a modest extension of DualDICE by introducing a linear successor representation to estimate marginalized weights. While the derivations appear sound, the empirical results do not convincingly demonstrate an advantage over existing deep TD algorithms. For a contribution that is primarily a practical variant of a theoretically grounded algorithm, a more compelling experimental evaluation is crucial. At present, the paper’s impact seems limited, as the benefits of the method are not clearly established.

reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
The paper proposes SR-DICE, which uses a successor representation to compute DICE (discounted stationary distribution correction term).
* I am worried about both the technical and experimental qualities of this work. The theorems presented are either obvious or previously presented in other works. While the authors argue that the marginalized importance ratio is independent of the horizon (I assume that they are talking about the variance), MIS only alleviates the estimator variance's exponential dependence on the horizon to become the polynomial dependence on the horizon (as proved in Xie et al., Towards Optimal Off-Policy Evaluation for Reinforcement Learning with Marginalized Importance Sampling, 2019). In the experiments, it is hard to believe that the GradientDICE and DualDICE perform that poorly having Log MSE larger than 0 while the GenDICE paper reports Log MSE less than -4 (HalfCheetah).
* The paper uses $\phi$ and $\psi$ learned by the previous deep successor representation learning algorithm, which is not meant to be used to learn marginal importance ratio. In particular, $\phi$ is learned by minimizing state, action, and reward reconstruction error and $\psi$ is the discounted sum of $\phi$. If we consider a case where $\pi$ only exploits a very small subset of state-action space, it is easy to see that the reconstruction error minimization in the dataset is not an optimal representation for the marginal importance ratio learning. In this sense, only the linear vector $w$ is used for the learning of marginal importance ratio.
* The experiment setting is not fair. Direct-SR and SR-DICE in their implementation have effectively 2 hidden layers, where DualDICE and GradientDICE in their implementation have a single hidden layer.
* The paper is hard to follow. Especially, notation abuse between the real reward and the virtual reward which is optimized to give a marginal importance ratio is very confusing (abuse between real Q and minimizer Q as well). Section 4.2 is also confusing because the authors imposes the problem of DualDICE that is not actually handled by SR-DICE.
* The idea of adopting successor representation for learning marginal importance ratio seems quite novel.
* Some people will be interested in this work, but I think the paper would not have much impact on the field.

Overall,

PROS:
* The idea of using successor representation for learning marginal importance ratio is novel.
* Avoids minimax formulation of other DICE algorithms, which makes the optimization very hard.

CONS:
* Not very meaningful theoretical results are presented, which mostly just confuse readers.
* Uses the representation that is not learned for marginal importance ratio learning
* Questionable experiment results


Minor details:
* y axis label is "Log MSE" for figures although the y axis is log scaled MSE.


--------------------------------------
Most of the concerns are addressed by the authors, and I raised my score accordingly.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review Summary  
This paper introduces SR-DICE, a method that employs successor representations to compute the DICE term (the discounted stationary distribution correction). Although the core concept is intriguing, I have significant reservations regarding both the theoretical contributions and the experimental evaluation.

Major Concerns  
1. Theoretical Novelty  
   - The paper’s theorems appear either straightforward or already established in prior work. For example, the claim that the marginalized importance ratio is horizon-independent seems to confuse “independence” with merely avoiding an exponential dependence on the horizon; as shown in Xie et al. (2019), marginalized importance sampling reduces the variance’s exponential growth to a polynomial rate, rather than eliminating horizon dependence altogether.  
2. Experimental Credibility  
   - It’s surprising that GradientDICE and DualDICE exhibit Log MSE values above 0 in these experiments, whereas the GenDICE paper reports Log MSE below –4 on the HalfCheetah task. This discrepancy raises doubts about the experimental setup or implementation fidelity.  
3. Use of Pretrained Successor Representation  
   - The model relies on φ and ψ derived from an existing deep successor representation algorithm designed to reconstruct state, action, and reward signals. These features were not optimized to estimate marginal importance ratios. In scenarios where the target policy π visits only a narrow slice of the state–action space, reconstruction-driven features may be suboptimal for ratio estimation. Effectively, only the final linear weight vector w is learning the true ratio.  
4. Unfair Comparison in Network Capacity  
   - In the experiments, Direct-SR and SR-DICE each employ two hidden layers, while DualDICE and GradientDICE use just one. This mismatch in model capacity undermines the fairness of the comparisons.  
5. Clarity and Notation Issues  
   - The manuscript is often hard to follow. In particular, the distinction between the real reward versus the “virtual” reward used to derive the marginal importance ratio—and similarly between the true Q-function and the minimizer Q—is muddled. Moreover, Section 4.2 introduces a shortcoming of DualDICE that SR-DICE does not explicitly address, adding to the confusion.  

Strengths  
* The application of successor representation to learn marginal importance ratios appears novel.  
* By avoiding a minimax optimization, SR-DICE sidesteps the difficult saddle-point problems characteristic of existing DICE variants.  

Weaknesses  
* The theoretical results presented lack depth and primarily reiterate known findings, which may mislead rather than enlighten readers.  
* The approach depends on a representation learned for reconstruction, not tailored for importance-ratio estimation.  
* The experimental results are questionable, given inconsistencies with prior literature and disparities in model architectures.  

Minor Comment  
– The y-axis label in the figures reads “Log MSE,” despite the axis being on a logarithmic scale.  

Conclusion  
While most of my concerns have been addressed in the authors’ response, I remain cautious about the technical novelty and empirical validation. Nonetheless, the idea is interesting enough to merit discussion in the community.  

Rating: 6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.original_text:
Review:
The paper proposes SR-DICE, which uses a successor representation to compute DICE (discounted stationary distribution correction term).
* I am worried about both the technical and experimental qualities of this work. The theorems presented are either obvious or previously presented in other works. While the authors argue that the marginalized importance ratio is independent of the horizon (I assume that they are talking about the variance), MIS only alleviates the estimator variance's exponential dependence on the horizon to become the polynomial dependence on the horizon (as proved in Xie et al., Towards Optimal Off-Policy Evaluation for Reinforcement Learning with Marginalized Importance Sampling, 2019). In the experiments, it is hard to believe that the GradientDICE and DualDICE perform that poorly having Log MSE larger than 0 while the GenDICE paper reports Log MSE less than -4 (HalfCheetah).
* The paper uses $\phi$ and $\psi$ learned by the previous deep successor representation learning algorithm, which is not meant to be used to learn marginal importance ratio. In particular, $\phi$ is learned by minimizing state, action, and reward reconstruction error and $\psi$ is the discounted sum of $\phi$. If we consider a case where $\pi$ only exploits a very small subset of state-action space, it is easy to see that the reconstruction error minimization in the dataset is not an optimal representation for the marginal importance ratio learning. In this sense, only the linear vector $w$ is used for the learning of marginal importance ratio.
* The experiment setting is not fair. Direct-SR and SR-DICE in their implementation have effectively 2 hidden layers, where DualDICE and GradientDICE in their implementation have a single hidden layer.
* The paper is hard to follow. Especially, notation abuse between the real reward and the virtual reward which is optimized to give a marginal importance ratio is very confusing (abuse between real Q and minimizer Q as well). Section 4.2 is also confusing because the authors imposes the problem of DualDICE that is not actually handled by SR-DICE.
* The idea of adopting successor representation for learning marginal importance ratio seems quite novel.
* Some people will be interested in this work, but I think the paper would not have much impact on the field.

Overall,

PROS:
* The idea of using successor representation for learning marginal importance ratio is novel.
* Avoids minimax formulation of other DICE algorithms, which makes the optimization very hard.

CONS:
* Not very meaningful theoretical results are presented, which mostly just confuse readers.
* Uses the representation that is not learned for marginal importance ratio learning
* Questionable experiment results


Minor details:
* y axis label is "Log MSE" for figures although the y axis is log scaled MSE.


--------------------------------------
Most of the concerns are addressed by the authors, and I raised my score accordingly.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold

reviews.confidence: 5: The reviewer is absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct and very familiar with the relevant literature
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
The authors propose SR-DICE based on deep SR for density ratio learning. Empirical advantages are observed in tested domains. Overall I think the idea is interesting and theoretically sound, but the experiments are not fully convincing.

It looks the main claim is that SR-DICE is better than other MIS methods because SR-DICE delegates the update propagation over the MDP to SR, while other MIS methods consider update propagation and density ratio learning together. To me this claim is not coupled with function approximation at all, so I would like to first see some experiments in the tabular setting. SR-DICE is a two-stage learning algorithm, i.e., SR learning + density ratio learning, both have hyperparameters to be tuned. GradientDICE and DualDICE are one-state learning algorithm. If in the tabular setting, we can empirically verify that under the best hyperparameter configuration of each algorithm (guaranteed by a thorough grid search), SR-DICE is more data efficient (counting the samples used in both stages) than GradientDICE and DualDICE, in terms of the density ratio prediction error, then the argument can be well backed. Well-controlled experiments like this, however, do not appear in the current submission.

Once deep networks are used for function approximation, we run into the problem of representation learning. The authors should at least include one more experiment, where MIS methods run directly on the pretrained deep SR features \psi_\pi(s) and/or \phi(s). In this way, we can distinguish whether the empirical advantage of SR-DICE comes from SR-DICE itself or the improved representation learning.

I'm also interested in seeing experiments for larger gammas, e.g., 0.999, 0.9999. I'm wondering if SR-DICE can consistently outperform GradientDICE with increasing discount factors.

Overall, I'm happy to increase the score if I have any misunderstanding or more convincing results are presented. I appreciate that the authors include deep TD and behavior R(\pi) as baselines. The empirical study has independent interest beyond SR-DICE. Moreover, deep TD is also referred to as Fitted-Q-Evaluation (FQE) in [x].

x: Voloshin, Cameron, et al. "Empirical Study of Off-Policy Policy Evaluation for Reinforcement Learning." arXiv preprint arXiv:1911.06854 (2019).

=======================

(Nov 24) The author response addressed my concerns and I therefore raised my score from 5 to 6. I particularly like the idea of using successor representation for density ratio learning.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review Summary  
The manuscript introduces SR-DICE, which leverages deep successor representations (SR) for estimating density ratios in off-policy evaluation. The core claim is that separating update propagation via SR from density ratio learning yields empirical gains over existing MIS methods that jointly handle these steps. While the idea is compelling and grounded in theory, the experimental evaluation requires additional evidence to fully substantiate the authors’ claims.

Major Concerns  
1. Lack of Tabular Experiments  
   - The key argument—that delegating propagation to SR leads to superior density ratio estimates—does not inherently depend on function approximation. To validate this claim, the authors should include well-controlled tabular experiments.  
   - In particular, since SR-DICE comprises two stages (SR fitting plus density ratio estimation, each with tunable hyperparameters) whereas GradientDICE and DualDICE update a single stage, a fair comparison requires a thorough grid search for each method’s hyperparameters. Under their optimal settings, SR-DICE should demonstrate greater data efficiency (counting samples across both stages) in terms of density ratio prediction error. Without these baseline results in the tabular setting, the central claim remains unverified.

2. Representation Learning Confound  
   - When moving to deep approximators, it becomes unclear whether SR-DICE’s improvements arise from the SR formulation itself or simply from learning a better representation.  
   - To disentangle these factors, I recommend an experiment where existing MIS methods (e.g., GradientDICE, DualDICE) are applied directly on fixed, pretrained SR features ψ_π(s) and/or φ(s). If SR-DICE still outperforms these transplanted baselines, we can attribute gains to the algorithm rather than representation learning alone.

3. Sensitivity to Discount Factor  
   - All experiments use moderate γ values. It would be informative to test higher discounts (e.g., γ = 0.999 or 0.9999) to see if SR-DICE consistently outperforms GradientDICE as the horizon extends.

Additional Comments  
- I appreciate that the authors compare against Deep TD and the behavior policy return R(π) as baselines—this strengthens the empirical contribution beyond SR-DICE itself.  
- Note that Deep TD is also known as Fitted Q Evaluation (FQE) in Voloshin et al., “Empirical Study of Off-Policy Policy Evaluation for Reinforcement Learning,” arXiv:1911.06854 (2019).  
- I particularly like the concept of employing successor representations for density ratio estimation.

Post-Author Response  
After reviewing the authors’ rebuttal and clarifications, my score has been raised from 5 to 6, reflecting improved confidence in the approach pending the recommended experiments.

Final Rating  
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold

reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
Strengths:

1) The experiments are extensive, and clearly demonstrate the merits as compared to prior benchmarks for off policy RL.

2) The contextual discussion is clear, well-motivates the proposed approach, and gives a nice overview of how importance sampling and off policy RL intersect.



Weaknesses:


1) Theorem 1 seems vacuous. The proof is a simple exercise in elementary calculus -- one may easily show the minimizer of a quadratic is the least squares estimator. The authors need to better explain what is the technical novelty of this statement, and why it is meaningful. Upon inspection it does not seem to qualify as a theorem. This is also true of Theorems 2 and 3. Therefore, I feel the conceptual contribution is not enough to warrant acceptance.

2) The notion of successor representation seems identical to the occupancy measure, which in order to estimate, requires density estimation, which is extremely sample inefficient. Can the authors comment about how to estimate the successor representation efficiently? There is very little discussion of sample complexity throughout, which is somewhat alarming because a key selling point of off-policy schemes for RL is that they alleviate the need for sampling from the MDP transition dynamics.

3) The actual algorithm pseudo-code is missing from the body of the paper, which is permissible because it is in the appendix. However, the structural details of how the algorithm works iteratively and how it departs from previous works are also not explained. That is, while derivation details are presented, iterative details are not. In my opinion this should be strictly required in the body of the paper, as well as contextual discussion of what is similar/different from previous works, but all I could find was high level presentation of objectives minimized at various sections, but not how they are interlinked.

4) The background discussion is disjointed. There is a preliminaries section on page 5, as well as a background section 3.


Minor Comments:


1) References missing related to the sample/parameterization complexity issues associated with importance sampling: 

Koppel, A., Bedi, A. S., Elvira, V., & Sadler, B. M. (2019). Approximate shannon sampling in importance sampling: Nearly consistent finite particle estimates. arXiv preprint arXiv:1909.10279.

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review:

Strengths:

1. The authors conduct a thorough set of experiments that convincingly outperform existing off-policy RL benchmarks.

2. The paper presents a lucid contextual background, effectively motivating the new approach and offering a concise synthesis of how importance sampling integrates with off-policy reinforcement learning.

Weaknesses:

1. Theorem 1 appears trivial. Its proof merely shows that a quadratic’s minimizer coincides with the least‐squares solution—an elementary calculus result. The authors should clarify the novel technical insight here and justify the significance of labeling it as a theorem. The same concern applies to Theorems 2 and 3. Given this, the theoretical contribution seems insufficient to merit acceptance.

2. The successor representation as described seems equivalent to the occupancy measure, whose estimation demands density estimation and is therefore highly sample-inefficient. The manuscript lacks a discussion of how one might estimate this representation more efficiently. More broadly, there is scant analysis of sample complexity—surprisingly absent for a work touting off-policy methods’ sample-efficiency advantages in RL.

3. Although the pseudocode for the algorithm resides in the appendix, the main text omits a step-by-step account of the iterative procedure and does not clearly distinguish this method from prior algorithms. While the derivations and objective formulations are laid out, the operational flow and interconnections among optimization steps remain unclear. In my view, a detailed description of the iterative routine and a comparative discussion against existing techniques should appear in the core paper.

4. The background material is fragmented: there is a “Preliminaries” section on page 5 and a separate “Background” section 3, creating redundancy and disrupting the narrative flow.

Minor Comment:

• The manuscript should cite work on the sample and parameterization complexity of importance sampling, for instance:
  – Koppel, A., Bedi, A. S., Elvira, V., & Sadler, B. M. (2019). Approximate Shannon sampling in importance sampling: Nearly consistent finite particle estimates. arXiv:1909.10279.

title: Exploring Zero-Shot Emergent Communication in Embodied Multi-Agent Populations

reviews.confidence: 3: The reviewer is fairly confident that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
The authors study the zero-shot emergent non-verbal communication in this paper. Different from most papers on emergent communication. this paper uses the motion of three-joint agents. The agents meet partners that they have never seen in the training phase, presenting the challenge of the universal protocol. To make a universal protocol possible, the authors study intents sampled from Zipf distribution and energy regulation. The authors conducted experiments on tasks with 2, 5, 10 intents. The results show that providing latent energy feature is essential for zero-shot coordination. To achieve better than chance accuracy on tasks with 10 intents, a torque curriculum is needed. 

Pros:
1. The setting of ZS coordination with non-verbal emergent communication is novel and interesting. 
2. The use of energy regulation and Zipf distribution is natural and intuitive. The results also support the intuition. 

Cons:
What is the aim of using motion as a communication protocol? In human communication, actions are often used as an iconic gesture to describe intents that are not well described by words. In this sense, humans can perform zero-shot non-verbal emergent communication since the iconic gestures are always universal. In other words, a grounded world should be used to study ZS coordination. This paper seems to study a problem that is not realistic.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review Summary:
This paper investigates zero-shot emergent non-verbal communication by having three-joint agents convey intents through motion. Unlike most emergent communication studies that focus on symbolic exchanges, here agents must coordinate with partners they have never encountered during training, which demands a universal signaling protocol. To facilitate such a protocol, the authors sample intents from a Zipf distribution and introduce an energy regulation mechanism. They evaluate their approach on tasks involving 2, 5, and 10 distinct intents and find that including a latent energy feature is crucial for successful zero-shot coordination. Moreover, achieving above-chance performance on the 10-intent scenario requires a curriculum on torque.

Strengths:
1. The proposed zero-shot coordination framework using non-verbal, motion-based communication is both original and engaging.
2. Leveraging energy regulation together with a Zipf-distributed intent sampling is conceptually straightforward, and the experimental findings convincingly back this design choice.

Weaknesses:
– It is unclear why motion is chosen as the communication medium. In human interaction, gestures often serve as iconic, universally understood cues when words fall short. Because these gestures are inherently grounded, humans naturally perform zero-shot non-verbal communication. This raises the question of whether an ungrounded motion-based setup realistically captures zero-shot coordination—suggesting the need for a grounded environment to make the study more applicable.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold

reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
##########################################################################

Summary:

The paper deals with agents that communicate non-verbally via actuating their joints in a 3D environment. The authors show that the agents should be able to learn protocols that can generalize to novel partners. Furthermore, the authors find that the current training approaches are brittle, and they propose and evaluate approaches to address this challenge.

##########################################################################

Reasons:

Overall, I vote for accepting. The authors develop and evaluate an algorithm that allows for emergent communication which generalizes to novel partners. Furthermore, the authors openly communicate and address the brittleness of the training.

##########################################################################

Pros:

* Novel algorithm to perform emergent communication via joint actuations
* Algorithm allows for generalization to novel partners
* Suggestions to improve training stability

##########################################################################

Cons:

* It would be desirable to train the agents N times with different initializations and report mean and std of the performance
* More experiments and evaluation results with various joint numbers, intents, potentially different communication strategies would be helpful 

##########################################################################

Misc:

The text in Figure 1 is too small.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review:

Summary:
This manuscript explores how agents can engage in non-verbal communication by moving their joints within a 3D simulation. The authors demonstrate that such agents can develop signaling protocols that transfer effectively to partners they have not encountered during training. They also uncover that commonly used training schemes are quite fragile, and they propose several methods to bolster stability, which they empirically assess.

Recommendation:
I lean toward acceptance. The paper presents a well-motivated algorithm for emergent, non-verbal communication via joint actuation that generalizes to novel collaborators. Moreover, the authors candidly address the brittleness of existing training regimes and offer concrete enhancements.

Strengths:
• Introduces a unique algorithm enabling emergent communication through joint movements  
• Provides evidence that the learned protocols generalize to previously unseen agents  
• Offers thoughtful strategies to improve the robustness of training  

Weaknesses:
• The experimental results would be more convincing if the authors repeated training with multiple random seeds and reported the mean and standard deviation of performance  
• Further empirical analysis—such as varying the number of joints, exploring additional intents, or comparing alternative communication schemes—would enrich the evaluation  

Minor Comment:
• The text in Figure 1 is too small and should be enlarged for readability  

Final Rating: 6 (Marginally above acceptance threshold)
